title: Assignments for Congestion-Averse Agents: Seeking Competitive and Envy-Free Solutions
abstract: We investigate congested assignment problems where agents have preferences over both resources and their associated congestion levels. These agents are \emph{averse} towards congestion, i.e., consistently preferring lower congestion for identical resources. Such scenarios are ubiquitous across domains including traffic management and school choice, where fair resource allocation is essential. We focus on the concept of \emph{competitiveness}, recently introduced by Bogomolnaia and Moulin [6], and contribute a polynomial-time algorithm that determines competitiveness, resolving their open question. Additionally, we explore two optimization variants of congested assignments by examining the problem of finding envy-free or maximally competitive assignments that guarantee a certain amount of social welfare for every agent, termed \emph{top-guarantees} [6]. While we prove that both problems are NP-hard, we develop parameterized algorithms with respect to the number of agents or resources.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies the congestion assignment problem. There are $m$ posts and $n$ agents, and the task is to assign each agent to exactly one post. The congestion of a post is defined as the number of agents assigned to it. Each agent will express a (not necessarily strict) ordinal preference over all (post, congestion) pairs, and the goal is to find a fair and efficient assignment.

The main result of this paper gives a polynomial-time algorithm for deciding whether a competitive (CP) assignment exists. In particular, an assignment satisfies CP if each agent neither envies another agent nor wants to be assigned to an empty post, and CP implies several fairness and efficiency notions to justify it as a desirable solution concept. This addresses an open question raised in prior work, which only shows that a CP assignment may not exist.

This paper also studies the hardness of deciding the existence of assignments satisfying properties implied by CP, such as envy-freeness (EF), top-guarantees (TG), and maximal competitiveness (MaxCP). It shows that deciding the existence of an EF and TG assignment is NP-complete, and finding a MaxCP assignment is W[1]-hard. The paper also complements these negative results by providing several fixed parameter tractable algorithms.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
This paper studies an interesting and well-motivating problem, witnessed by a large volume of literature on the congestion assignment problem and its variants. Also, the solution concept focused by this paper, competitiveness (CP), is arguably prominent as it directly implies several fairness and efficiency properties, and hence it is important to enhance our understanding on it. The proofs in the paper are correct to the extent that I checked.

It appears as a surprise to me that the existence of a CP assignment can be verified efficiently since the same task tends to be computationally intractable for many other solution concepts, as also supported by the negative results in this paper. The algorithm is also non-trivial: it exploits the observation that one can reduce the task to verifying the existence of a CP assignment where all posts are non-empty, and then it gradually adjusts the congestion vector in search of the desired assignment. I believe this result will be of great interest to the community.

While I can see that the authors have done a considerable amount of work on improving the writing, and I really appreciate their effort, there is still room for improvement (see the following comments). Overall, this is an interesting and solid paper, and I would support its acceptance, provided the presentation will be improved.

Detailed comments:
- Line 44: "$n$ and $m$" -> "$n \times m$".
- Line 46: Given that the sorting algorithm is quite easy to state, it would be better to briefly describe it, say, in the footnote.
- Line 47: Briefly define in the footnote what Borda utilities are.
- Line 75 Q2: The existence of TG and EF assignments is not guaranteed, so phrasing the question as "finding a TG and EF assignment" is confusing.
- Line 75 Q3: Maximal CP has not been defined up to this point.
- Line 83: In my first read, I misunderstood being filled as being full instead of being non-empty. It would be better to either explain the definition of being filled here or change "filled" to "non-empty".
- Line 120-123: The claim on the hardness for finding a CP assignment with lower and upper quotas deserves further elabotation, say, in the appendix, since the connection does not seem very straightforward.
- Line 172: "satisfied" -> "unsatisfied".
- Line 213: Typo "Section Subsection".
- Line 247: The statement here seems to conflict with (iii) in Construction 2.
- Line 280: When defining $f$ to be an integral function, it would be better to explain that this is without loss of generality since the capacities of the graph are all integral.
- Line 282: "$(x, t)$" -> "$(x, t) \in E(G)$".
- Line 331: As mentioned above, stating "it is NP-hard to find such assignments" is inappropriate since the existence of such assignments is not guaranteed.

QUESTIONS:
1. Does Theorem 6 directly imply Theorem 4? If yes, then Theorem 4 seems redundant here.

2. What are the challenges of generalizing Algorithm 1 to the weighted case where the congestion of a post is the total weights of the agents assigned to it?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper considers post assignment problems with congestion: A is a set of posts (e.g., job postings), and V is a set of agents. Each agent can be assigned to one post, a post may consist of many agents, and the number of agents at a post is the congestion. Agents have preferences over the tuples A x [n], or, posts combined with the congestion at that post.

They discuss a number of desirable properties in the general area of fairness or stability: Nash stability (NS, no agent should desire to deviate from their post), non-wastefulness (NW, no agent should desire to deviate to an empty post), envy-freeness (EF, no agent should desire to swap with another), top-guarantee (TG, any agent must receive one of their top tuple assignments), and competitiveness (CP, non-wastefulness and envy-freeness). These properties have been previously studied, and it is known that CP=>NS and NS=>TG.

PROPERTIES
They show a number of nice properties useful in their main algorithm, but these results stand on their own: CP => EF and NS, but the reverses (respectively) aren't true, NS => TG+NW but the reverse isn't true. Additionally a number of these properties are incomparable.

MAIN ALGORITHM
Their main result closes an open question posed by a very paper in the field: is competitiveness P-time solvable? They describe a rather involved algorithm which does so. The main algorithm consists of two main nested loops. In the outer loop, they iteratively check all values k denoting the number of empty posts.

For a given k, they attempt to compute a competitive assignment. They first reduce the instance with k empty posts to an instance with no empty posts (and show that solutions to these two instances correspond to each other). Given such an instance, they then iterate (in the inner loop) over a sequence of guessed congestion vectors, which bounds the congestion at any post. At first, the vector is highly constrained: each post can only have 1 assigned agent.

Given a fixed congestion vector, first they check to see if an assignment can be made satisfying the vector. If so, that is a valid solution and they are done. Otherwise, they identify a number of congestion constraints implied by the vector that are necessarily too weak. This is determined using a max flow technique and reasoning similar to Hall's theorem. These congestion bounds are increased by 1, and the loop continues. We stop when the congestions sum to above the number of agents present - e.g., it's impossible to find a solution. This completes the algorithm.

HARDNESS
They show that a simultaneously EF+TG solution is NP-complete but also FPT wrt either n or m.  Finding the max CP solution that is also TG is W[1]-hard wrt t, in XP wrt t and m, and FPT wrt n. Deciding if there exists an assignment with at most t unsatisfied agents is W[1]-hard wrt t. Not much detail is given beyond this in the main body of the paper.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
STRENGTHS: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The technical results are advanced and require a combination of techniques. New results are provided on both the hardness and algorithmic side of the problem, which involve closing an existing open question in literature.

WEAKNESSES: Multiple of the results are under-explained, likely due to space. However I really would have liked more than a list of theorem statements at the end of the paper for much of the hardness section. I think it is more important to give a high-level understanding of all components of the paper than to squeeze in what proofs (or components of proofs) you can fit at the detriment of explanation. It is difficult to get any sense of where these results come from, let alone check for correctness.

QUESTIONS:
1. On line 275, should the capacities be T[a] not T[1]?

2. Could you add a proof of Observation 1? I'm pretty sure it's correct, but observations should be immediately obvious. I'm not sure I would even call this an observation, or more of a small lemma.

3. How novel are your results on page 7? You mention they are similar to Hall's theorem - does this not surmount to a b-matching version of Hall's theorem? I'm sure that's already been studied, though I don't know the citation off-hand.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies *congested assignment problems* involving agents with preferences not only over resources (posts) but also over the congestion levels of those resources. The agents are assumed to be *congestion-averse*, meaning they prefer fewer co-users on the same resource. The study focuses on various fairness criteria, particularly **competitiveness (CP)**, **envy-freeness (EF)**, **Nash stability (NS)**, **top-guarantees (TG)**, and **Non-wasteful (NW)**, where CP implies EF and NS, and NS implies TG and NW.

The primary contributions are:
- A **polynomial-time algorithm** to decide whether a *competitive assignment* exists, resolving an open problem posed by Bogomolnaia and Moulin (2025).
- Proofs that finding assignments that are both **top-guaranteed** and **envy-free**, or **top-guaranteed** and **maximally competitive**, is **NP-hard**.
- Development of **parameterized algorithms** for the aforementioned NP-hard problems, focusing on parameters like the number of agents, posts, or unsatisfied agents.

In short, this work gives both theoretical insights and algorithmic contributions for fair resource allocation under congestion-averse preferences.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Although the work is not groundbreaking, it addresses a natural and well-motivated model, and successfully resolves several important problems in this domain. The paper is clearly written and easy to follow. I do not identify any significant weaknesses. A minor point is that the central concept builds upon ideas introduced in prior work.

QUESTIONS:
I would like to double-check my understanding of the concepts presented in the paper:

1. If an assignment satisfies **envy-freeness (EF)** and **non-wastefulness (NW)**, does it satisfy **competitiveness (CP)**?

2. If the answer to (1) is yes, would it be reasonable/meaningful to consider the combination of **Nash stability (NS)** and **maximal competitiveness** as an alternative objective?

3. Are there any immediate implications or known results connecting these fairness concepts (EF, NW, CP, NS, etc.) with **Pareto optimality** within this model? (No need for new results—just existing ones or trivial ones, if any.)

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies congested assignment problems in game theory and shows a poly-time algorithm that determines whether there is a competitive assignment (resolving an open question of Bogomolnaia and Moulin). The paper also shows that finding envy-free or maximally competitive assignments is NP-hard, but shows parameterized algorithms for these problems with respect to the number of agents or resources.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- Well written paper that resolves a recent open question with a nice reduction to network flow
- Shows that finding other kinds of assignments that may be natural to think about for this problem is NP-hard
- Shows FPT algorithms for these NP-hard problems
- Nice related work section that places this work in context
- The problem itself (assigning agents to resources, with cost functions associated with each resource depending on the number of agents assigned) feels natural, hopefully indicating that this work could have broader consequences. The notion of a competitive assignment also seems natural, and it is quite interesting that competitive assignments can be found in polynomial time, while other kinds of assignments that one may want (Envy free, top guaranteed) are NP-hard.

Weaknesses:
- Could elaborate on some practical applications of the proposed algorithms -- it would be nice to indicate some real-world settings where this problem could be applied, and where the algorithm proposed may be relevant. Some suggestion is made that this could be applied to settings like urban traffic -- expanding on this, and perhaps presenting some initial experiments could significantly strengthen the work.
- It would also be interesting to elaborate on further potential consequences of this work to nearby areas -- for example, should one expect this dichotomy between the polynomial time computability of competitive assignments and NP-hardness of envy-free and top-guaranteed assignments to be a broader phenomenon outside of just this particular setting? What are the technical challenges involved in generalizing the results here to the weighted agents setting? 

Overall I am impressed with the clarity of the work and technical results. However, I am not an expert in game theory, and have very little to no experience in the area. I am also skeptical of the practical applicability of the results, which seems like a downside for a conference like NeurIPS.

QUESTIONS:
- Are there any immediate practical applications of the algorithms proposed?
- Could you empirically validate these algorithms?
- How would you introduce and motivate this work to audiences outside of algorithmic game theory? What is the significance of this work to other areas of CS/theory?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
After discussion the reviewers all found this paper satisfactory with no major outstanding issues, although perhaps some room to improve the presentation.  On this positive side, there is a consensus that the paper makes solid technical contributions in resolving an open question in the theory of fair division and is of a quality suitable for publication.  On the negative side, the topic addressed in relatively specific within the context of fair division and the paper doesn't break any substantial new ground.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
